data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Optimism Bumps Up
data.description:
Optimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, neutral sentiment and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 6.8 percentage points to 28.5%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 13th time in 15 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 3.8 percentage points to 12.5%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 38th time in 40 weeks. Neutral sentiment was last lower on May 28, 2009, (11.0%) and was the 14th-lowest reading in the survey’s history.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 3.0 percentage points to 58.9%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 19th time in 21 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 9.7 percentage points to –30.4%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 14th time in 16 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they would describe the current valuation of stocks.

Here is how they responded:

  • Stocks, in general, are overvalued: 29.2%.
  • Stocks, in general, are fairly valued: 13.0%.
  • Valuations are mixed, with some stocks expensive and others cheap: 36.4%.
  • Stocks, in general, are undervalued: 14.5%.
  • Not sure/no opinion: 6.9%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 28.5%, up 6.8 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 12.5%, down 3.8 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 58.9%, down 3.0 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

If you want to become an effective manager of your own assets and achieve your financial goals, consider a risk-free 30-day Trial AAII Membership.

data.title: The Medium-Term Impact Of Tariffs On The Technology Sector
data.description:
Since the Trump administration announced the tariffs, over $5 trillion has been wiped out of the US financial markets. First of all, I want to say this: the stock market has no ideology, no belief, no idea. The biggest reason I love the stock market and became a financial analyst is that the stock market is a place where the truth hits you in the face.

If you look at the stock markets of developing countries, you can see this very clearly. The closer a country gets to justice, logic, science, security and democracy, the more its stock market gains value. The biggest reason for this is that trust in the country increases, uncertainty disappears, and therefore investment begins to enter the country.

Although this difference is not clearly visible since markets such as the US and Europe are already mature, developments such as the tariff events we are experiencing these days remind us of this aspect of the stock market again and again.

When looked at from a completely rational economic and financial perspective, these tariffs unfortunately seriously increase the uncertainty of the general market in the country and also restrict the mobility of the companies that produce the majority of the country's GDP. We can easily see this uncertainty and restriction by looking at the stock market. Yes, we all know that the market is on an upward trend in the long term, but the upward trend in the long term does not mean that short-term political decisions are actually correct; on the contrary, if a decision has a negative effect on the stock market, that decision may unfortunately not be correct.

In this article, I will not focus on whether the political decisions made are right or wrong, but on how they are reflected in the market and what kind of impact they have on which companies. My aim is to examine the tariff exposures of the stocks that have the largest share in most investors' portfolios and to help them make more accurate investment decisions.

META: Meta Platforms, Inc.

META has shown superb performance since 2023. It appears to be able to insulate itself from the production tariffs to some extent during this difficult period. Despite this, there are inevitable difficulties. I think it is necessary to focus on Chinese advertisers, especially those targeting US consumers because the biggest impact of these tariffs on META will be the slowdown in advertising revenues.

Senior advertising analyst Brian Wieser estimates that about $10 billion of META’s U.S. revenue comes from international advertisers, primarily from China. So, in this case, Chinese advertisers would be expected to reduce their ad spend significantly due to the higher costs of doing business in the U.S. market.

With the tariffs on the agenda, META has lost around 30% of its value from $730 on February 14 to $510. I think the impact of the tariffs on META will be limited.

GOOGL: Alphabet Inc.

Although Alphabet faces a complex mix of challenges from the new tariff landscape, I think it will weather the storm better than some of its competitors.

While GOOGL's search engine and YouTube advertising businesses are somewhat insulated by their local-focused algorithms, I think the broader economic impact of the tariffs can reduce overall ad spending, which is similar to META but only slightly. The real issue is GOOGL’s cloud side.

The cloud business is facing increasing costs for data center expansion and equipment. Trump’s tariffs, particularly on China, Taiwan and South Korea, of more than 30% mean that critical resources for data processing machines and components are being cut off. It seems that if there's any response from these countries, that would make the situation even worse.

While the company manufactures its own TPUs, as I noted in my previous GOOGL article, I think the data side will still be the most exposed to these tariffs. The company fell by around 30% from its peak of $207 to $144. In conclusion, I think the impact of the tariffs on GOOGL is limited unless additional tariffs are imposed.

AAPL: Apple Inc.

AAPL is likely to be one of the most directly and severely affected by Trump’s tariffs due to its heavy reliance on Asian manufacturing. AAPL’s business model makes it extra vulnerable to these tariffs because “substantially all” of its manufacturing takes place in China, India, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan and Vietnam. As a result of that, I expect it to face serious supply chain and cost issues.

Morgan Stanley analyst Erik Woodring says AAPL may need to raise prices across its product line by 17-20% in the U.S. to offset the tariff costs. The company faces a dilemma here, including a margin squeeze and potential volume hit. It’s a situation no company wants to face.

While some have noted that AAPL has been actively diversifying its production beyond China in recent years (iPhones made in India and AirPods coming from Vietnam), the fact that these alternative production countries are also affected by the new tariffs unfortunately does not leave AAPL much room to escape. Not only that, but the App Store restrictions covering the EU and China also seem to undermine its service revenues.

AAPL has fallen from $250 to $170 with a 32% decrease in its price tag following the tariff announcements, and unlike other companies, it has profit and supply chain problems in the medium term. The impact of the tariffs on AAPL is really big and has a high potential to slow down the company's growth rate.

MSFT: Microsoft Corporation

MSFT is also one of the companies that is less affected by the tariff regime. Similar to GOOGL, MSFT’s area of ​​impact is primarily its cloud infrastructure. MSFT’s Azure infrastructure is based on global data centers equipped with hardware from tariff-affected regions such as China. The increase in component costs means eroding margins in a critical growth segment for a company that is seeing 33% annual growth in Q1 2025.

There is a risk that enterprise customers will reduce their IT spending if tariffs slow global growth. This could lead to companies opting for cheaper in-house solutions instead of Azure. This potential indirect impact could hurt MSFT’s growth.

Around 68% of MSFT’s cloud and software revenues are US-based, making the company heavily insulated from global trade wars. Because of this isolation, the company fell 23% to $345 after the tariff news, unlike the 30% declines of other giants. I think the impact of these tariffs on MSFT will be limited.

AMZN: Amazon.com, Inc.

AMZN is facing problems in both its e-commerce and cloud computing departments due to Trump’s tariffs. On the retail margin front, if AMZN can’t take remedial measures such as cost reductions or supplier negotiations, its U.S. merchandise expenses could increase by 15% to 20%.

Goldman Sachs analyst Eric Sheridan also estimates that AMZN could see $5 billion to $10 billion in annual operating profits due to higher first-party merchandise costs due to the tariffs.

Chinese merchants make up half of AMZN’s largest U.S. marketplace sellers and are a major driver of the company’s advertising revenue. There is a significant risk that these merchants will reduce their U.S. presence and advertising spend in response to the tariffs.

While the retail business faces significant challenges, I believe AMZN’s scale, scalability and ability to drive results for advertisers could provide some resilience in the advertising segment.

Amazon Web Services (AWS) faces similar challenges to other cloud providers, but AWS and local storage in the US still provide revenue diversification for AMZN. The tariff news sent AMZN shares down 29% from $240 to $170. I think the impact of the tariffs on AMZN in the medium term will be less than the impact on AAPL, but more than the impact on GOOGL.

NVDA: NVIDIA Corporation

NVDA was one of the most talked about stocks during these tariffs because NVDA was already playing a big role in the economic war between the US and China before the tariffs. Although NVDA has fallen much more than other tech giants after the tariff announcements, I don't think it's doing that badly.

First of all, the fact that semiconductors are clearly exempt from the “mutual” tariffs is a small positive, but it's unfortunately unclear how much this will reduce the negative impact on costs. It's clear that there will be disruptions in supplies from China. But definitely the investments brought by the CHIPS and Science Act really reduce the damage that the tariffs do to NVDA, especially in the long term.

About 60% of NVIDIA’s DGX and HGX AI data center servers are manufactured in Mexico, potentially avoiding the recently implemented US tariffs due to exemptions under the USMCA trade agreement.

Since the tariffs were announced, NVDA has fallen by 40% from $140 to $86. I think the tariffs will hurt NVDA, at least in the short to medium term. In addition, NVDA is one of the main players in the economic war between the US and China, and that's why it can be exposed to new tariffs, which adds extra risk to the stock. As someone who added NVDA to his portfolio as its price fell, I mentioned in my NVDA article that I am confident in the company’s future. I will continue to add it to my portfolio, but I must say that it is at great risk.

ASML: ASML Holding N.V.

ASML's EUV and DUV lithography machines exported to the US are not exempt from the announced tariffs, but since it is a monopoly power in technology, its profit margins are somewhat protected. The company's demand elasticity for its products is very low, so it will most likely pass on the cost increases to its customers.

The company's decline since the summer of 2024 is already known to those who have it in their portfolio. With the addition of tariffs to this decline, ASML has fallen by up to 26% since the beginning of 2025, falling to $590. I think the impact of tariffs on ASML is limited.

TSM: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited

TSM, like other semiconductor manufacturers, benefits from the exemption of semiconductors from reciprocal tariffs, despite Taiwan’s 32% general tariff rate. The company has committed $165 billion to the U.S. for three new factories, advanced packaging facilities, and an R&D center in Arizona. The investment, described by the White House as “the largest foreign direct investment in U.S. history,” significantly reduces the company’s vulnerability to future trade action. Perfect timing.

Despite the tariff exemptions, TSM remains exposed to broader China-Taiwan geopolitical tensions that could disrupt its operations. The company’s exceptionally important strategic location for global technology supply chains puts it in the spotlight, like NVDA in the U.S.-China war.

I think that TSM, which has fallen 36% from $210 to $134 since the tariffs were announced, will not be affected as much as expected as a chip manufacturer in the medium to long term. The impact of the tariffs will be limited, especially due to supply chain diversification.

MA: Mastercard and V: Visa

Initially, concerns were largely focused on the fact that tariffs would squeeze global trade, which can reduce MA and V’s transaction volumes. I see this as likely.

So, there are concerns, but both companies focus on local consumption rather than cross-border trade, which provides significant resilience to direct tariff impacts. Their transaction-based revenue models continue to work independently of the price of the goods purchased.

V is down 13.58% and MA is down 15.60% since the tariffs were announced. I think the tariffs will have a very limited impact on these companies.

Conclusion

In conclusion, it is a fact that the tariffs applied by the US to most countries in the world also negatively affect the companies that provide a large portion of its own GDP. Although these effects vary from sector to sector, the uncertainty of tariff policy makes it difficult to diagnose risk. Despite this, I conclude that APPLE is the riskiest among the giants, while MA, V and MSFT are the least risky technology giants. Although the fall in the stock market provides an opportunity to add new stocks to our portfolio, it is best to evaluate this opportunity by considering the risks.

data.title: I Bonds: Chaotic Times Provide A New Motive To Buy This Ultra Safe Asset
data.description:
With the CPI number which came out at 8:30 Eastern Time today, Thursday, April 10, it's now possible to calculate the exact return an investor will receive for the next year. It's as if you could choose to buy or not buy a stock or fund for what it sold for a year in the past. The way this works is based on the way I Bond rates are reset on May 1 and November 1. Let's take May 1 as an example as it's close to the upcoming reset on May 1, 2025, three weeks from today. You have a full 21 days to decide if you want to buy the current I Bond, (although you might choose to do so a few days earlier so you don't run the risk of your purchase not being processed before May 1).

Here's a table showing you how this works:

Nov 1, 2024 RESET Oct24 Nov24 Dec24 Jan25 Feb25 Mar25
YOY % Change 2.6% 2.7% 2.9% 3.0% 2.8%

2.4%

 6 month change
MOM% Change 0.12 -0.05 0.04 0.65 0.44 0.22 1.43
May 1, 2025 RESET Apr24 May24 Jun24 Jul24 Aug24 Sep24
YOY% Change 3.4% 3.3% 3.0% 2.9% 2.5% 2.4% 6 month change
MOM% Change 0.39 0.17 0.03 0.12 0.08 0.16 .95

The first thing to notice from the table is that the March year-over-year CPI-U inflation number at the upper right is the number reported today, April 10. The year-over-year number - usually the headline in the press - is 2.4%. The core rate was 2.8%. The month-over-month number just below the year-over-year number is less reported, but more significant as it is more accurate. It is the number that sums to the variable rate on I Bonds and is 0.1 higher than the sum of the individual months, which compound monthly. Note also that the April 10 report is, obviously, for the March CPI number as, of course, the month of April still has three weeks to go. For that reason, the year for May 1 resets run from March 31 through October. CPI through September and vice versa. It's always a good idea to buy I Bonds near the end of the month as a purchase at any time in the month pays interest starting at the first of the month, just don't make it too close to the last couple of days.

Also be aware that the real (fixed) rate changes at each reset and the fixed rate at the time of purchase becomes the long term fixed rate for the life of the Bond, which can be as much as 30 years. The fixed rate will be announced on November 1, set by the Treasury according to criteria which have never been disclosed but generally are close to two thirds the rate on the 5-year nominal Treasury Note. The current fixed rate is 1.2%, but there is a possible but not certain lowering to 1.1%. Either would still be a good fixed rate based upon the long history of I Bonds. The fixed rate is of course an important factor in your decision to buy or not buy because it sticks around so long, while the variable rate changes every six months when the variable rate is reset.

If you buy before May 1 you will receive the current fixed rate of 1.2% in perpetuity. The current composite rate will continue at 3.11% for six months, then move to 4.08% for the following six months. That's competitive with many shorter duration rates at this time.

The Recent History Of I Bonds

The recent history of I Bonds had quite a bit of melodrama. For a couple of years, their extraordinary performance in the high inflation from June 2021 through July 2023 made I Bonds the talk of the town. The lowest inflation rate over that period was 5% and the highest was just over 9% for a month. Several months had a CPI over 8%. I Bonds are normally the dullest investment you can imagine. That suits me fine. They are first and foremost a way to hedge against serious inflation - a sort of insurance policy in case inflation goes wild, as it did from 2022 through 2023. I just buy them and forget them, although I am aware that others buy I Bonds for a variety of reasons. To cite just one, they are a good parking place for money when other vehicles are offering very little return. The most recent such low return period was from 2009 through 2020 when the Fed suppressed rates and banks and other savings vehicles were happily able to pay depositors next to nothing in interest.

Starting in late 2021, however, I began to inform anyone who would listen about what a great asset I Bonds are when there is high inflation. Who could beat them for an asset that was entirely without risk, guaranteed by the U.S. Treasury, and full of deal sweeteners like great flexibility such as the freedom to choose your own exit point anywhere from 1 to 30 years, foregoing one quarter of interest if exiting before holding for five years.

Among the more esoteric perks that came with I Bonds at that time was the Gift Box option, which made it possible for relatives and close friends to double up by buying each other "gifts" to double or potentially more than double the $10,000 limit for individuals. When I first heard of this through a comment by a reader who was commenting on one of my articles, it sounded too good to be true, but I immediately did the necessary research and found that it was absolutely true. I doubled down with my stepson for a couple of years, using part of it as a way of making him an actual gift in exchange for his learning how it worked on the site and walking me through it.

With all the activity and sudden interest in I Bonds, it didn't surprise me that there were a few times when so many buyers rushed to buy around the semi-annual resets that the whole system was overwhelmed with customers and especially customers (like me, I guess) who needed help with some problem on the site. On one occasion, numbers were so great that many purchases couldn't be processed before the reset date, leading to great dissatisfaction of some buyers. It was clear that TreasuryDirect hadn't anticipated such a rush to such a very dull investment and simply wasn't staffed to keep up with the demand.

Times changed. Inflation gradually became less severe, at least for now. As the hyperinflation subsided, I Bonds lost their mojo and faded into relative obscurity. There was even some talk about them being phased out (don't worry, it's unlikely to happen any time soon). I Bond investors stopped going to TreasuryDirect in such numbers that the site was overwhelmed and had to work out how to deal with investors who submitted requests in time but couldn't be processed before the end of the month. As far as I know, all investors with that problem were eventually given the proper date. That all ended abruptly when inflation rates finally began to normalize in the middle of 2023. Who needed an inflation-protected U.S. Treasury Savings Bond with which you can buy only $10k per year when the real return on ordinary U.S. Treasury Bonds was becoming very competitive?

Inflation seemed to be on a steady path to the much advertised Federal Reserve target of 2%. Yields on ordinary Treasury Bills, Notes, and Bonds were on the upswing. For the first time in years, the I Bond fixed rate was lower than that of their chief direct rival, U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Bonds (abbreviated as TIPS), which had returned much less than I Bonds for many years. As a result, I Bonds became an afterthought. As time passed, the enthusiasm for I Bonds faded. I actually wrote in my last I Bond article that I Bonds had served me well over almost 25 years, but I was letting go of them as my portfolio was large enough.

I Bonds appeared to have surrendered their special niche, but then an unexpected thing happened. Inflation had fallen to 3% in June 2023 but then seemed resistant to falling farther. You can see this if you look carefully at the above table. As recently as January of this year it was right back at 3%, falling to 2.8% in February, although some savants thought that the strong underlying PCE rate suggested higher numbers in the future. In short, the Fed thought the normal rate for inflation should be 2%, but inflation had a mind of its own. Just this week, the Fed expressed reluctance to start dropping interest rates in the face of a likely recession. The only possible interpretation of that view was that inflation continued to be a Fed concern. Meanwhile, the I Bond fixed rate had risen a bit to 1.2% making I Bonds once again more than competitive with non-inflation-protected Treasuries and TIPS.

Along Came Tariffs And A Double Threat

Then along came tariffs and with them an unpredictability beyond anyone's wildest imagination. The one major advantage that I Bonds had over both regular and inflation-protected bonds was defense against both inflation and deflation. For the first time in years, I Bonds now deserve a place in every portfolio. This feels like a moment to stop and provide a more complete definition of I Bonds and their unusual advantages.

We tend to forget that inflation and deflation are sort of the coequal evil twins of the financial world. Over the long term, 100 years, inflation has had the better of the contest with an average around 3% depending on the point in time where you choose to start the series. There's one period each in which their particular form of economic poison was dominant: the 1930s for deflation and the 1970s for inflation. Other outbreaks were brief and usually resulted from major wars, after which there was a spike in inflation followed by a downward spike of deflation like a stalactite and a stalagmite. Because the 1970s were recent enough that many people still remember them as a personal experience, inflationary periods are thought of as the greater threat, but deflation cannot be ignored as a possibility under present circumstances. You slow down inflation simply by jacking up rates as high as it takes but ending deflation is much tougher. It usually requires a war. The Smoot-Hawley tariffs of 1930 were a major cause of that decade's deflation and many economists believe that it was thus a major cause of the Depression. It ended as the world built armaments for WWII.

So which is it now? One possibility is that it is both, first inflation as prices of foreign goods go up, then deflation as unemployment increases and consumers sink prices by buying less at lower prices. Can I Bonds do anything to hedge against serious deflation? Yes and no. The best hedge may be hiding cash under the mattress, but that implies giving up positive returns altogether. That worked during the Great Depression of the 1930s if you happened to have any money to hide under your mattress. And what if inflation and deflation come and go at intervals? I Bonds do their part on both sides if inflation and deflation are sporadic. If inflation turns suddenly into deflation, I Bonds lock in all past periods of returns except the present six months. As for a given six-month period used for resets, the loss as a result of a deflationary environment is limited to zero in the composite return.

Negative inflation numbers have happened just twice since I became involved with I Bonds. On May 1, 2015, the variable rate was -0.80% but as the fixed rate was zero, there was no deflationary impact. That was a win of 0.80% for I Bond investors. The second occasion was May 1, 2009, when deflation was a negative 2.78% but cost investors just the 0.10% fixed rate, thus saving I Bond investors 2.68%. This locked all but 0.10% of past interest into place so that it resumed virtually where it left off at the next reset. I Bonds thus respond to deflation in a much better way than TIPS, which dock the immediate deflationary moment as it happens on a monthly basis. It doesn't help TIPS either that interest earned must be paid annually in IRS taxes, while I Bond holders can postpone taxes for up to 30 years. They can thus be paid in greatly devalued dollars.

At the end of this article I provide the same appendix as I always do, almost word for word, in every I Bond article. If you feel that you already understand I Bonds in detail, you may ignore the Appendix, but if you need to refresh your memory, I suggest that you jump to the Appendix and read it now. It's about a five-minute read if you want to slow down and be sure you're getting everything. Here's a paragraph from that Appendix explaining the advantage I Bonds have in defending against both inflation and deflation:

One of the most important but seldom noticed qualities of I Bonds is that they're not only an excellent inflation hedge but a hedge against deflation as well. The reason is that the composite return never dips below zero. In the event of a deflationary period, the accrued inflation value of the I Bond would simply freeze at its highest level. When inflation returns, as it always has, the total value of the account begins to compound again from the highest level it has reached."

Just understand that I Bonds aren't an investment designed to make you rich. They play a small but potentially meaningful role in enabling you to stay rich and be assured that you own a bit of the one super safe asset which keeps up with inflation and defends against deflation. I have used them for that purpose for 25 years without ever being disappointed, and will detail my experience in the next section.

25 Years Of Positive Experience With I Bonds

My 25-year experience with I Bonds can be divided into four separate phases, each of which was highly satisfactory. In each phase, I Bonds served important needs of that particular moment in time. Much of the impact of I Bonds is psychological. I never cashed an I Bond to make up for the rising price of groceries or other things I needed. What they provided was the knowledge that I had one investment that to a degree counteracted inflation. Somehow, that made me feel that inflation had less bite.

1. I Bonds were first issued in 1998. The concept was that they were to be a sort of "poor man's TIPS." I missed the first two years because I was focused on other ways to duck the oncoming dot.com collapse, but by 2000 I had familiarized myself with them and saw that they could have a role as a safe asset with a good return that was the antithesis of dot.com stocks. In the beginning, you bought paper I Bonds at banks. I bought the $30K max which came with a fixed rate of 3.60% and a variable rate of 1.91% for a composite 5.51% yield, a real return and composite return that seemed too good to be true. It was. TreasuryDirect quickly realized that they had been too generous in both the amount you could buy and the fixed rate, and lowered both. That was fine. If you buy a large chunk of an interest paying asset early and in size, it continues to overpower smaller additions as time goes by. I bought quite a bit more despite lower fixed rates until the maximum dropped to $10K. I kept buying most of the time until 2012 when only electronic purchases were available, and I began buying that way.

2. Starting in 2008 when the Fed was busy suppressing any safe income sources for conservative investors and older people like me, I discovered that inflation could actually be your friend if you owned I Bonds. Even though the fixed rate was often minuscule, zero for about 75% of the time over 10 years, the variable inflation rate was higher than the yield on most money market funds and the like. The short-term interest rates were around 2% for the two decades starting in 2000. That debases the dollar to a surprising extent, but it was a pretty good thing for an investor looking for a safe yield while the Fed was having a long term nervous breakdown over the economy.

3. The inflation which started in late 2021 through 2023 was a moment of glory for I Bonds. Everybody wanted I Bonds. My first year's purchase in 2000 yielded just under 13%. Their long-term value quadrupled and a bit more, a roughly 6% yield over a little more than 20 years for an ultra-safe asset. I started thinking of my I Bonds as a portfolio made up of a particular and very helpful asset class.

4. A couple of years ago, I decided to pause buying I Bonds as my portfolio was already quite large. At that time, I was busy trying to decide which of my assets should go to which of my children, consistent with their needs and abilities. My daughter was the future inheritor of I Bonds. I realized, however, that those wonderful years 2000 I Bonds would roll off at maturity in 2030 when I would be 86, and I decided to keep buying I Bonds with my daughter in mind, being sure to write a page of instructions explaining what she needed to learn about them. I also provided funds for her to buy I Bonds specifically for her and her children. This had the helpful effect of requiring her to learn how to navigate the site of TreasuryDirect. The truth is, there's always likely to be a specific use for I Bonds if you think about it.

Conclusion

Pretty simple. Continue to consider buying I Bonds. I will tell you right off that I bought my annual quota of $10K I Bond for the year day before yesterday because I am absent-minded and might have missed the date. I will add that I had to be sure I could speak to a TreasuryDirect phone answerer because I had been locked out of my account. It happened because I had misspelled Hemingway, which was one of the answers to personal questions necessary to allow me to change my password in order to conform with the new password criteria.

(A Side Note: I know perfectly well how to spell Hemingway but failed to strike the letter "g" hard enough on my computer. I even know that there is a Hemingway restaurant here in Oak Park, a very good bistro, which spells its name with a double M in order to avoid litigation. Try the Beef Wellington if you are a red meat eater, or the whitefish if you are not. Pretty darn good foie gras too according to my wife. Wellington was, of course, the never defeated British general who took down Napoleon.)

I provide the detail on my password change because it's easy for an easily distracted person like me to make that kind of mistake on the site, and sometimes very hard to get anyone on the phone to help you fix it. For this reason, I proceed slowly and check and recheck to be sure that I know what I am doing when taking an action at the TreasuryDirect site. If you do make a mistake, as I did, just call the number provided and be prepared to leave time to wait a half hour or more before a human voice answers (although the phone periodically says the wait time is 15 minutes). The person who answered yesterday was very nice, thorough, and patient.

It's not a bad idea to take a look at the Appendix that follows, as a few things have changed since the last time I edited it. Leave comments and questions. I'll do my best to answer all of them coherently, and will even look up answers.

Appendix: What Are I Bonds And How Do They Work?

I Bonds are a form of US Treasury Savings Bond which is inflation-adjusted. Like ordinary EE Savings Bonds, I Bonds are backed by the United States Treasury. Savings bonds have been around since 1935. Under a temporary name change to War Bonds, US Savings Bonds helped fund World War II. I Bonds have been around since 1998.

I Bonds produce returns with two components. The first is a fixed rate which provides the "real" return - real meaning inflation adjusted. For new purchases, this rate resets semiannually on May 1 and November 1, or the next business day if the first day of the month happens to fall on a holiday. Once you purchase an I Bond, the real rate in force at that time continues for 30 years until the bond matures or until the owner chooses to redeem it. The second component of I Bond return is the inflation rate as represented by the Urban Consumer Price Index (CPI-U), the same index that is used for Social Security resets. The inflation rate used for I Bonds is reset semiannually at the same time the fixed rate is reset, on May 1 and November 1, and the accrued value of your I Bond is updated so that the return compounds semiannually. Note that the fixed rate and the variable rate are reset every six months, the variable rate from the CPI report, the fixed rate at the whim of the Treasury, which has sometimes been thought to track the five-year Treasury Note.

You can currently buy up to $10,000 annually of electronic I Bonds in a TreasuryDirect account - that's $10,000 per Social Security Number so that you can buy the same amount for your spouse and each of your children. In the past, you could also request that up to $5000 of your Federal income tax refund be received in the form of a paper I Bond which will be mailed to you whenever the IRS gets around to sending refunds. That possibility was phased out, however, on January 1, 2025.

Like all Treasury Bonds, I Bonds are not taxable by states and municipalities. Another advantage of I Bonds is that you can choose to defer paying Federal taxes until maturity or redemption. This can be helpful if you or your heir (whom you designate with each purchase) expect to have lower taxable income at some point in the future. Under some circumstances, I Bonds are tax-free when used for educational purposes - read the fine print, though.

One of the most important but seldom noticed qualities of I Bonds is that they're not only an excellent inflation hedge but a hedge against deflation as well. The reason is that the composite return never dips below zero. In the event of a deflationary period, the accrued inflation value of the I Bond would simply freeze at its highest level. When inflation returns, as it always has, the total value of the account begins to compound again from the highest level it has reached.

I Bonds are probably the most flexible investment available. This is because their duration is any date you choose, from one year to their maturity date of 30 years. You cannot redeem your I Bond during the first year, but after that, you can choose any moment you wish if for any reason you want to get out. You can thus use them as a place to park cash for the short to intermediate term, or leave them in place and continue to have an assured long-term hedge against inflation.

For the first five years, the price paid for redeeming a bond is giving up one quarter (three months) of returns. After five years of owning an I Bond, the forfeiting of one quarter's returns no longer applies. There are, by the way, no commissions or other costs involved in buying or selling I Bonds. I Bonds can be bought at price intervals from $50 to $10,000 while electronic bonds may be bought in penny increments starting at $25. You could, for example, buy an I Bond with a face value of $25.23. The Treasury Direct site has a slightly more detailed description.

It's worth a mention that there is likely something going on concerning the use of the Gift Box. Some I Bond holders (not me) were surveyed about their use of the Gift Box. Earlier, there had been messages to users (again, not me) "requesting" that they Deliver all Gift Box holdings promptly. There seemed to be no way of compelling anyone to do this (I didn't) along with some issues about the preferences of the Recipient. I will update if TreasuryDirect works out an actual policy. For now, I would ignore any request which has no means of enforcement.

data.title: Bloodied But Unbowed, Agilon Health Can Profit From Improving Market Dynamics
data.description:
Investment Overview

agilon health (NYSE:AGL), a provider of primary care physician (PCP) services mainly to Medicare Advantage health insurance plans, is bucking the turbulent bear market trends to date in 2025. At the time of writing, its shares are up over 30% in the past week and over 165% year-to-date. The current share price is $5.20, and the market cap valuation is $2.14bn.

On a more negative note, shares are down ~77% since agilon's IPO in April 2021, raising a stunning $1.2bn at a price of $23 per share. In June 2021, the stock briefly traded at over $40 per share, but it has been mostly downhill ever since. This is probably due to a couple of factors.

Firstly, financial performance—as we can see below (source: Seeking Alpha), agilon's revenue growth has been strong, but it is a heavily loss-making company:

The company achieved revenues of over $6bn in 2024, but made an operating loss of $(284m), reporting GAAP earnings per share (EPS) of $(0.63).

Secondly, the Medicare Advantage industry, which enjoyed a reputation for being the fastest growing, most popular with patients aged 65+, and most lucrative for health insurers, began to lose its way in 2023 and through 2024, as healthcare utilization rose faster than plan providers had expected and the Centers for Medicaid and Medicare Services (CMS) raised its payment rates to plan providers by a lower-than-expected amount, putting significant pressure on profit margins.

As recently as October last year, analysts at Citi downgraded agilon stock, lowering its price target from $7, to $2.50, citing headwinds in the Medicare Advantage (MA) industry. Although agilon stock fell as low as $1.60 per share in November 2024, it has come roaring back, and spiked yesterday as the CMS hiked its rate payments for 2026 to +5%, compared to 3.7% for 2025, and more than double what it had proposed in January.

The key question is whether Medicare Advantage is enjoying a long-term resurgence, or whether this may be a false dawn. In this post, by diving a little deeper into agilon's business and outlook for the sector, I'll try to answer that question.

agilon Business Overview – MA Focused Proponent Of Value-Based Care

It's no secret that health insurers have a difficult relationship with the public, and despite the advantages that MA plans offer, such as dental care and gym membership, the fact that it drove gross margins nearly twice as high as, e.g. individual market or Medicaid plans for providers, arguably made it the most disliked of all.

Perhaps that partly explains why much of agilon's 2024 annual report/10K submission reads more like a manifesto for better healthcare than a business description, but we can tease out some passages that help us understand how the business operates and how it makes money.

Let's begin with a business overview, as per the 2024 10K:

Our business model combines the agilon platform, a network of like-minded physicians and a long-term partnership model to provide physician groups with the necessary capabilities, capital and business model to create a Medicare-centric, globally capitated line of business.

We enter into contractual agreements with health plan payors in each of our geographies, under which we are financially responsible for our physician partners’ provision of a defined spectrum of healthcare services to our members, in exchange for a defined PMPM fee for each of our members (which is also referred to as “global capitation”).

In essence, MA plan providers can use agilon's network of participating physicians to administer care to patients on its behalf, in exchange for a fee. agilon says:

The global capitation fees we are entitled to receive from our health plan payor contracts are typically based on a defined percentage of the corresponding monthly premium payments which the payor receives from CMS for members attributed to our PCPs and covered under such contracts.

agilon is a proponent of “value-based care” (VBC) models as a long-term replacement for "Fee For Service" (FFS). As the company explains:

Under a traditional FFS reimbursement model, physicians are paid a fixed amount for services provided during a patient visit, regardless of a patient’s medical need or health outcome.

As a result, physician reimbursement is solely related to the volume of patient visits and procedures performed, thereby offering limited financial incentive to focus on preventative care and cost containment.

Value-based care models offer alternative reimbursement models, which typically incentivize physicians for improving the cost and quality of healthcare provided for an attributed patient population.

Various types of value-based care reimbursement models exist, including capitation, bundled payments, or payments for attainment of improved quality metrics or medical cost efficiency.

As for the size and scale of the company, agilon says:

Our company was formed in 2016, and we established our inaugural partnership with an anchor physician group in 2017. Our ability to rapidly build scaled positions in local communities has allowed us to grow to 29 anchor physician groups and 30 geographies as of December 31, 2024.

Our platform has enabled us to grow our total membership by 36% and revenue by 40% from December 31, 2023 to December 31, 2024. As of December 31, 2024, the PCPs on our platform serve approximately 526,500 MA members and 132,100 Medicare fee-for-service (“FFS”) beneficiaries through ten Accountable Care Organizations (“ACOs”) through our participation in the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services’ (“CMS”) Accountable Care Organization Realizing Equity, Access, and Community Health (“ACO REACH”) Model and Medicare Shared Savings Program (“MSSP,” and together with ACO REACH, the “CMS ACO Models”) through its equity method investments.

2025 Guidance For Fewer Members, Lower Revenues?

Below is the guidance agilon has provided for 2025 (source: Q4 earnings presentation):

An obvious question is: Why agilon is forecasting for fewer members at the end of 2025, and lower revenues also? The answer is that MA began to grow less and less profitable through 2024, owing to the previously discussed increase in healthcare utilization, and the lower than expected rate rise from the CMS, who pay private companies, i.e., UnitedHealth (UNH). CVS Health (CVS), Humana (HUM), Cigna (CI), Centene (CNC) and others besides to administer MA plans.

These companies pocket the difference between the amount they spend on healthcare, and the amount they receive from the CMS, which is often referred to as the medical benefit ratio, and expressed as a percentage (healthcare expenditure divided by premiums received).

agilon refers to a “medical margin”, or "medical services revenue after medical services expense is deducted", which was just $1m in Q4, and $205m for the full-year 2024, management revealed on its Q4 earnings call with analysts. The reality is that many of agilon's MA members became unprofitable in 2024, and management advised on its earnings call that:

Based on current market dynamics, we have made a strategic decision to constrain our 2025 MA membership to balance near-term risk and opportunities. And we now anticipate a full-year MA membership decline of approximately 4% or 22,000 members to a range of 490,000 to 520,000 or 505,000 at the midpoint.

This year-over-year change includes; adding 20,000 members in a smaller class of 2025 from 3 new partners, same geography growth of 3% or 13,000 members and a reduction of 54,000 members from previously disclosed partnership exits, multiple December 2024 payor contract terminations and tighter attribution management with health plans.

Management advised that this will help it achieve 46% medical margin growth to $300m in 2025, although we may also note that cherry-picking the most lucrative plans only, and abandoning less profitable plans, is one of the traits that makes health insurers so unpopular with the public.

agilon's insists its model is working, however, stating on the earnings call that:

Current MA results show our readmission, hospital admission and ER visit rates 20% to 30% better than the local fee-for-service benchmark and quality scores for each year 2 plus market approaching or greater than 4.25 stars.

The star rating is important, as the CMS pays bonuses to any MA plan that is rated four stars or higher; hence, by improving the star rating, agilon demonstrates its value to its healthcare payor clients. More generally, agilon told analysts:

Specifically, with our physician partners, we are better connecting opportunities across our burden of illness, quality and care delivery programs to drive best-in-class medication adherence, further address advanced illnesses like palliative care and target significant high-acuity chronic disease categories like heart failure, dementia and COPD, in which the primary care physician is positioned to intervene earlier with the goal of preventing disease progression, alleviating symptom burden and avoiding unnecessary ER and inpatient utilization.

This is arguably the most positive element of the "VBC" approach—keep patients' healthier by providing better care, and theoretically, the profits will take care of themselves.

Looking Ahead – 2025 Is A Reset Year For MA – Profitability By 2027 A Possibility

After a turbulent year for MA, almost as a direct result of which CVS stock fell from $80 per share to less than $45 per share, and Humana stock fell from over $500 per share, to under $250 per share, health insurers are referring to 2025 as a “reset” year.

It's very clear that in 2024 there was a major disconnect between the CMS, health insurers, and the public, and if MA is going to survive as a viable model going forward, this must be addressed in 2025. The CMS has arguably done its bit by rubber-stamping a +5% rate rise in 2026, although we may note that the CMS increased its rate based on its own internal risk calculations, and not in response to lobbying from health insurers.

Now agilon needs to do its bit by providing the high standards of healthcare that are supposed to be its hallmark, and if it can do that, it ought to attract more business from payors, and meet its own goal of being "cash flow breakeven by 2027".

Furthermore, the change in government in the US is perceived by some analysts to be a net positive for MA and for companies like agilon. Citi upgraded agilon stock in January, stating that:

We do think there is potential that a Trump administration and CMS (likely led by Dr. Mehmet Oz) implement more Medicare Advantage-favorable policies.

It looks increasingly likely that Dr. Oz will end up leading the CMS, and that he will likely be favorable towards MA, and less favorable towards, e.g. Medicaid and the Affordable Care Act marketplace; however, we may note that Citi only upgraded its price to $2.25, and that the Trump administration will be looking to save costs to fund tax cuts, and one area it could look at is payments to MA.

CMS remains convinced that it is paying out too much to plan providers, to the tune of billions of dollars per annum; however, a recent attempt to claw back ~$2bn from UnitedHealth Group fell flat.

Concluding Thoughts – A Trickier Environment Than Expected, But agilon May "Make Hay While The Sun Shines"

Back in 2021, CVS paid over $10bn to acquire Oak Street Health, a company that is similar to agilon in many respects, operating a network of physicians with the aim of reducing the costs of providing healthcare for MA patients.

Oak Street was losing even more cash than agilon and I questioned the wisdom of the deal at the time. Oak Street likely played a small part in CVS' disastrous 2024 which saw the company downgrade guidance and fire its CEO and the head of its health insurance division, as it consistently missed earnings targets.

That is not the greatest advertisement for agilon's business model, but frankly, if it can't mount a comeback in 2025 and 2026 when its share price trades at a fraction of its IPO price, then there is probably no hope for this business in the long term, and no hope of a lucrative (for shareholders) exit to a health insurer.

That said, there are enough tailwinds—downgraded expectations, exiting unprofitable plans, a more MA friendly government administration, higher rate increases, and more experience of the markets—to suggest that agilon can have a solid year in 2025, and a successful year in 2026, so I'd say I am bullish at the present time.

My “guesstimate” would be, if agilon does break even in 2027, it could be worth $10 per share, or over $4bn, which doesn't necessarily flatter a company generating over $6bn in revenues, with +500k MA members served, and bearing in mind the price tag for Oak Street back in 2021.

By the end of 2025, or let's say by the time guidance for 2026 is provided, I don't think a share price of ~$7.50 would necessarily flatter agilon, which implies there may be 25% more upside for the stock to realize across the next 12 months.

data.title: Verastem: RAMP-203 Study Could Be Next Big Win For Avutometinib Combination
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Verastem, Inc. (NASDAQ:VSTM), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Verastem: Other Catalysts In 2024 Beyond NDA Rolling Submission Of Defactinib." In the article, I noted about the prospects of using avutometinib to be used in several combinations to treat patients with KRAS G12C mutant non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC] in a few studies. Such studies noted then were phase 1/2 studies RAMP-204 and RAMP-203. An update with respect to this program advancement is that the RAMP-204 study using avutometinib + adagrasib [KRAZATI] to treat patients with this type of mutant NSCLC was discontinued.

It was not because of safety issues or anything. Rather, the discontinuation was because it chose to continue with the development of the RAMP-203 study. This phase 1/2 study uses the combination of avutometinib + LUMAKRAS [sotorasib] with or without defactinib for the treatment of patients with KRAS G12C NSCLC [Both previously treated or untreated with a KRA G12C inhibitor]. Initial data showed 2 of 3 patients to initially respond to treatment, with all 3 having continued to be on therapy at the time of data release. This was from data released from this phase 1/2 RAMP-203 study back in December 2024. If this data was released, then why do I believe it is ideal to go over this biotech again? It is because there are two major key catalysts to be on the lookout for in 2025.

The first of which is that the company is expected to release an interim data update from this study using avutometinib + LUMAKRAS with or without [+/-] + defactinib to treat these KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC patients at a medical conference in the 2nd half of 2025. Secondly, another solid update was achieved, which was that the FDA not only accepted the New Drug Application [NDA] submission of avutometinib + defactinib for the treatment of patients with KRAS mutant low-grade serous ovarian cancer [LGSOC] but gave it a Priority Review Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] action date of June 30th of 2025. What's even more bullish about this is that the FDA noted that it was not planning to hold an advisory committee meeting at this time to discuss this regulatory application.

Avutometinib Presses On For A Possible Win In Another Indication

As I noted above, Verastem has been doing very well in continuing development of its drug avutometinib + defactinib for the treatment of patients with recurrent low-grade serous ovarian cancer [LGSOC]. This is with respect to the company having its NDA accepted by the FDA for review under an Accelerated Approval pathway, with a PDUFA date set for June 30th of 2025.

However, what I want to go over here is the continued possibility of the company achieving a positive outcome for another type of cancer. This would be with respect to its program development of using oral RAF/MEK inhibitor avutometinib with Amgen (AMGN) LUMAKRAS [sotorasib] for the treatment of patients with KRAS G12C mutant non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC]. One thing to immediately note is that the doublet of avutometinib + LUMAKRAS may or may not have FAK inhibitor defactinib added to it. Before going over this phase 1/2 RAMP-203 study, plus any catalysts to be released for this program, I believe that it is important to go over what NSCLC is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC] is a type of disease whereby there is the growth of malignant cells that form a tumor in the lung. In particular, this is a more common type of lung cancer, accounting for approximately 85% of cases. In addition, the cancer cells themselves under a microscope appear to be larger and less uniform compared to that of small-cell lung cancer [SCLC]. Another item to note is that a majority of NSCLC cases comprise of those who have gotten it due to smoking. It is said that smoking is responsible for about 80% of cases. That's not to say that those who don't smoke can't get it, but they are less likely to.

Some symptoms that these patients with NSCLC experience are as follows:

  • Chronic cough in place
  • Fatigue
  • Hoarseness of the throat
  • Shortness of breath
  • Coughing up blood.

The global Non-small cell lung cancer market size is projected to reach $36.9 billion by 2031. However, the company has a focus on only targeting a specific group of patients. In essence, to target NSCLC patients who have the KRAKS G12C mutation. This is still actually a good thing, because of the population that can still be targeted. The reason why is because the KRAS G12C variant is the most common KRAS mutation in NSCLC, accounting for approximately 13% of cases. The hope is that avutometinib + LUMAKRAS plus with or without defactinib is ultimately able to establish a superior efficacy profile compared to that of KRAS G12C inhibitors in development or for which have already been approved.

In order to see if the avutometinib doublet or triplet combination is going to be able to help treat patients with KRAS G12C mutant Non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC], Verastem is currently running the ongoing phase 1/2 RAMP-203 study. This open-label trial is expected to recruit up to 153 patients who are to be treated with either the doublet or triplet of avutometinib as follows:

  • Avutometinib + LUMAKRAS
  • Avutometinib + LUMAKRAS + defactinib.

This trial is deployed as an open-label type, but it has dual co-primary endpoints of the assessment of dose-limiting toxicities [DLTs] to find the recommended phase 2 dose [RP2D] and confirmed overall response rate [cORR] per RECIST1.1 over a 16-week period.

There is a catalyst opportunity for investors to look forward to here, which is that Verastem plans to release an interim data update on both the doublet and triplet combinations, treating these patients with KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC, at an upcoming medical meeting in the 2nd half of 2025. Whether or not positive data is released then remains to be seen. However, what has been shown thus far in terms of preliminary data is, to say the least, quite encouraging. In updated data released back in December 2024, from several patients, it was revealed that there were no DLTs in the first triplet combination cohort. Not only that, but 2 out of 3 patients treated with this avutometinib triplet combination had achieved initial 20% tumor reductions at first scan. All 3 patients continued to be treated with this triplet as of the cut-off date of November 21st of 2024. These were not easy patients to treat at all.

While this phase 1/2 RAMP-203 study was set up to target both previously treated and untreated KRAS G12C therapies, the focus of these first 3 patients was those who had progressed on prior KRAS G12C treatment [previously treated]. As was the case with the RAMP-301 study, adding FAK inhibitor defactinib to the RAMP-203 study contributed to an enhanced anti-tumor response in these patients with KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC. The addition of defactinib didn't just happen at random. Nope, it was preclinical data done by the company itself that brought it to the decision to add this drug to the doublet of avutometinb + LUMAKRAS. In preclinical testing, it was noted that the addition of an FAK inhibitor increased anti-tumor efficacy of the avutometinib doublet combination. This was observed in KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC models. Another observation made then was that for tumors that don't respond to a G12C inhibitor, can be made to respond again when an FAK inhibitor is added to the regimen.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Verastem Oncology had cash, cash equivalents, and investments of $88 million as of December 31st of 2024. However, the company enacted several financial transactions, which helped it to bring in additional cash into its coffers. Such transactions were:

  • $75 million of notes and $7.5 million of equity to be purchased by Oberland at closing of deal
  • Repayment loan with existing Oxford Finance of $42.7 million
  • Net proceeds generated from equity issuance under the company's at-the-market facility in January 2025 of $22.7 million.

With all of these other transactions done afterward, the company then had a pro forma cash basis of $151.3 million. The company burns roughly $31.6 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $20.8 million in R&D expenses and then $10.8 million in SG&A expenses.

With only the $88 million in cash, the company's financial runway would have been bleak, with it only expecting to fund its operations for at least 12 months. However, as I have shown above, it was able to nearly double the amount of cash on hand, which greatly extends its cash runway. It believes that through the agreements made that it should have enough to reach commercialization status of avutometinib + defactinib for the treatment of patients with recurrent LGSOC. Of course, that's only if this avutometinib combination is approved by the FDA on or before June 30th of 2025.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Verastem Oncology. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing development of the phase 1/2 RAMP-203 study, which is using avutometinib + LUMAKRAS + defactinib for the treatment of patients with KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC. While the company provided a good preliminary update showing that 2 out of 3 patients were able to achieve a 20% tumor reduction on first scan, there is no assurance that another batch of patients will achieve a similar or superior efficacy outcome. Another risk to consider is that even with somewhat compelling data from this RAMP-203 study, there is no guarantee that the company will be able to move forward toward the next phase of clinical development.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the NDA of avutometinib + defactinib for the treatment of patients with recurrent low-grade serous ovarian cancer [LGSOC], being accepted by the FDA with a Priority Review PDUFA date of June 30th of 2024. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this avutometinib + defactinib combination is going to be approved by the FDA for the treatment of these patients. Another risk to consider is that even if approval is given for U.S. marketing, there is no assurance that a substantial amount of revenue will be generated for it.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of an expansion opportunity that this company is undertaking. That is, it is also exploring the use of avutometinib + defactinib in combination with chemotherapy to treat patients with 1st-line metastatic pancreatic cancer in an ongoing phase 2 study. The chemotherapy in question is what is typically used for these patients, which is a gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel regimen. The company is expected to present additional data from this study at a medical meeting in mid-year 2025. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the data to be presented will end up being positive, nor cause the stock price to trade higher.

Conclusion

Verastem Oncology has done well to continue to advance a few drugs from its pipeline. Most notably, it has been able to establish that the addition of defactinib as an FAK inhibitor together with avutometinib has shown to increase anti-tumor responses in patients with certain types of cancer. The very first program to accomplish such a feat was in terms of the development of avutometinib + defactinib for the treatment of patients with recurrent LGSOC.

With that said, there is a huge catalyst coming up, which is the established Priority Review PDUFA date of June 30th of 2025. Then, the FDA will decide if this avutometeinib combination is going to be approved to treat these specific ovarian cancer patients.

Besides this, the whole purpose of this article was to discuss the company potentially being able to have a successful outcome in using avutometinib for another indication beyond LGSOC, which is treating patients with KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC patients. This program is coming along well because the company released initial positive data from 3 patients to show initial response.

Not only that, but the company is expected to release additional data from this RAMP-203 study at an upcoming medical meeting in the 2nd half of 2025. With the PDUFA date of avutometinib + defactinib for the treatment of patients with recurrent LGSOC patients established for June 30th, 2025, plus the release of updated data from the RAMP-203 study in the 2nd half of 2025, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: The GLTR ETF Continues To Shine
data.description:
Gold reached new record highs over the past weeks, and silver broke above critical technical levels on the long-term chart before correcting. Platinum and palladium prices have been trading on either side of the $1,000 per ounce level.

Markets reflect the economic and geopolitical landscapes, which have been highly uncertain. Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) markets have experienced waves of buying during these uncertain times.

The abrdn Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLTR) holds physical gold, silver, platinum, and palladium bullion, with over 90% invested in gold and silver. I last wrote about GLTR on Seeking Alpha on January 2, 2025, concluding:

Many investment professionals recommend holding 5% to 10% of portfolios in precious metals. With gold and silver in bullish trends and platinum and palladium consolidating and offering significant value below the $1,000 per ounce level, GLTR could be an excellent growth asset to tuck away in portfolios for 2025.

GLTR was trading at $111.64 per share on January 2 and has moved higher with gold and silver prices over the past months.

Gold rises to new highs

After rising 27.47% in 2024, COMEX gold futures added to last year’s gain, moving another 18.24% higher in Q1 2025.

The quarterly chart highlights gold’s ascent to a new record high of $3,201.60 per ounce in early April. Gold has reached a new all-time peak in each month of 2025.

With gold in uncharted territory, the next upside target is the inflation-adjusted 1980 $875 per ounce high.

The chart shows that gold’s 1980 high translates to nearly $3,400 per ounce in 2025, which could be the yellow metals’ net milestone.

Silver has been volatile

While gold has made new record highs in each of the past three months, silver remains substantially below its all-time highs.

Silver’s quarterly chart shows the second-leading precious metal broke above the 2024 $35.07 high in March 2025, when the price reached $35.495 per ounce. The next upside target is at the February 2012 $37.58 peak, which could be a gateway to a challenge of the 2011 and 1980 $49.82 and $50.36 per ounce highs. Silver fell to a $27.545 low in early April as risk-off tariff-related selling hit markets across all asset classes. Silver prices plunged as precious metals were excluded from tariffs. Uncertainty over the tariffs had caused silver to move from Europe to the U.S. The exclusion caused a downdraft, but silver recovered, trading around $31 per ounce on April 10, 2025.

Stable price action in platinum group metals

Platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) and palladium (XPDUSD:CUR) are the other precious metals trading in U.S. futures exchanges.

The quarterly chart shows platinum prices remain far below the 2008 record high of $2,308.80 per ounce. Platinum has been consolidating around the $1,000 pivot point for a decade and was under the $1,000 level on April 10, 2025.

Meanwhile, the quarterly palladium futures chart illustrates the price plunge from the record 2020 $3,425 high to below the $900 level.

South Africa and Russia are the world’s leading platinum group metals-producing countries. Moreover, gold and silver are far more liquid metals with higher daily trading volumes and open interest, the total number of open long and short positions in the futures markets. When the consolidation period in the leading PGMs ends, low liquidity could exacerbate price volatility, leading to parabolic rallies in platinum and palladium as they have lagged gold and silver over the past years.

The GLTR ETF is a diversified approach to physical precious metals

The most direct route for investing in precious metals is the physical market for bars and coins. Gold, silver, platinum, and palladium futures offer alternatives to the physical metals. Meanwhile, the four precious metals have liquid ETF products. While GLD, IAU, BAR, and other ETFs hold physical gold bullion, SLV owns physical silver. PPLT, PLTM, and PALL hold platinum and palladium bullion. Meanwhile, precious metals mining companies that explore for the metals and extract them from the earth’s follow the price path of the metals, with the miners often outperforming on the upside. Mining companies provide leverage and tend to underperform the metals during downside corrections.

The abrdn Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF owns a portfolio of four precious metals:

As the chart shows, GLTR owns gold, silver, platinum, and palladium, with a positioning that reflects liquidity. Gold is the most liquid precious metal, so GLTR allocates over 68% to the yellow metal. Silver is second, with a 25% allocation. Platinum and palladium, with far less liquidity in the futures and physical markets, have a combined 6.92% allocation.

At $126.72 per share, GLTR had $1.27 billion in assets under management. GLTR trades an average of 60,154 shares daily and charges a 0.60% management fee.

The monthly chart highlights GLTR’s 20.6% gain in 2024. In Q1 2025, GLTR moved 17.9% higher.

The twenty-year chart shows that GLTR has made higher lows and higher highs since late 2015. The ten-year bullish trend remains firmly intact in Q2, 2025.

The case for higher precious metals prices over the coming months

The compelling case for owning precious metals includes:

  • Inflation remains above the Fed and other central bank’s 2% target level, supporting higher precious metals prices.
  • Central banks remain net gold buyers, validating the yellow metal’s increasing role in the global financial system.
  • Over the past years, the geopolitical turmoil and bifurcation of the world’s nuclear powers have supported the ownership of precious metals as an integral part of investment portfolios.
  • Currency depreciation supports precious metals, the world’s oldest hard currencies.
  • Gold’s inflation-adjusted high from 1980 remains above the current price level.
  • Tariffs are trade barriers that impact currency levels, making precious metals attractive. Moreover, tariffs have caused metals to move from Europe and other wholesale exchanges to the U.S., tightening supplies.
  • Over the coming months, the market could experience an expatriation wave, with countries bringing their gold home from central depositories like the Bank of England and U.S. Fed, further tightening supplies.
  • Country debt levels have skyrocketed, depressing currency values against precious metals. Gold has rallied in all currency terms.
  • The U.S. dollar’s role as the world’s reserve currency could diminish, further supporting gold and other precious metals as a means of exchange.
  • The trend in any market is always your best friend. Gold’s trend has been higher since the 1999 bottom, and silver has been in a bull market since the 2020 pandemic-inspired low.
  • Platinum and palladium are inexpensive compared to gold and silver. Therefore, the rallies could be parabolic when the current consolidation trends break from around the $1,000 level.

While GLTR’s management fee receives a C-, risk at B- and liquidity at B+ are passing grades. The A+ in momentum reflects the bullish price action.

The bottom line is that precious metals should be integral to investment portfolios. China just approved a pilot program allowing insurance companies to invest in gold, which could cause demand to soar. GLTR is a liquid ETF product that allows investors easy access to a portfolio of the four precious metals through the stock market, as the ETF trades on NYSE Arca.

Demystify commodities in an era of uncertainty

Commodities experienced significant volatility in 2024, with energy prices impacted by geopolitical tensions, while precious metals and industrial metals saw significant gains. Looking ahead to 2025, the outlook remains murky, with trade policies shaping future commodity performance. During these times, you need someone who can share their knowledge and expertise to position for success.
